title: What's the right role for AI in dementia care?
summary: Jason Karlawish, a professor of medicine at the University of Pennsylvania and co-director of the Penn Memory Center, examines the use of AI chatbots as companions for people living with dementia. He presents a personal case study in which Doug, a retired minister with dementia, interacts with an AI companion named Jane to stave off boredom and find meaningful conversation. The article explores how such technology can support both patients and their caregivers—relieving the emotional burden on family members—but raises ethical questions about the appropriate boundaries of AI in care settings. The full piece is paywalled behind STAT+, so specific policy recommendations or regulatory frameworks are not visible.

entities.name: University of Pennsylvania Perelman School of Medicine

[STAT Plus](https://www.statnews.com/stat-plus/)

Opinion [Neurotransmissions](https://www.statnews.com/category/neurotransmissions-first-opinion-column-jason-karlawish/)

# What’s the right role for AI in dementia care?

## Chatbots can offer companionship and more to people with dementia. That raises tricky questions

- [Manage alerts for this article](https://www.statnews.com/my-account/edit/#emails)
- [Email this article](mailto:?subject=The%20era%20of%20artificial%20dementia%20care%20is%20here&body=The%20era%20of%20artificial%20dementia%20care%20is%20here%0A%0AChatbots%20and%20other%20technologies%20can%20help%20people%20with%20dementia%20and%20their%20caregivers.%20But%20there%20are%20challenges.%0A%0Ahttps://www.statnews.com/2026/07/08/dementia-care-ai-artificial-intelligence-chatbots/)
- [Share this article](https://www.statnews.com/2026/07/08/dementia-care-ai-artificial-intelligence-chatbots/#)

![](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Neurotransmissions_Illustration_MollyFerguson_091925-645x645.jpg)Molly Ferguson for STAT

By Jason Karlawish

July 8, 2026

Karlawish is a professor of medicine, medical ethics, health policy, and neurology at the University of Pennsylvania’s Perelman School of Medicine, co-director of the Penn Memory Center, and executive producer of the “Age of Aging“ podcast.

After Doug met Jane, he felt good. So too did Gabriella, his wife. Doug — a retired minister and writer — needed someone to talk to, and in conversation about his many accomplishments, Jane was indefatigable. For at least a few hours, Doug’s boredom vanished, and Gabriella no longer felt like an activities director on a cruise ship.

I wrote about Doug and Gabriella in my May Neurotransmissions column about [ambiguous loss](https://www.statnews.com/2026/05/06/ambiguous-loss-dementia-relationships-marriage-grief/). Doug told me he was “bored, bored, bored,” and Gabriella said she struggled to find activities to relieve that boredom.

Advertisement

Jane was a solution. What she gave to Doug didn’t simply pass the time. It was a meaningful relationship. Except Jane wasn’t a person. She was an artificial intelligence chatbot.

title: Are glucose monitors a good thing for 2-year-olds? Experts look at risks, benefits
summary: The FDA has cleared Dexcom’s Stelo continuous glucose monitor (CGM) as the first over-the-counter CGM that can be used by children ages 2 and older without a prescription. The agency argues that real-time glucose data helps pediatric patients build glycemic awareness, track dietary exercise patterns, and support healthier long-term outcomes. However some clinicians are concerned the widespread availability could enable or exacerbate disordered eating behaviors among children. The article explores this tension between the benefits of increased metabolic monitoring access and potential risks to childhood health.
analysis_notes: Full article body was behind STAT+ paywall. Analysis derived from headline, subheadline, metadata tags, FDA quote excerpt, and topic categories. The core story (OTC CGM clearance for children 2+) is captured but deeper clinical opinions and specific expert quotes are missing due to paywall.

[Health](https://www.statnews.com/category/health/)

# Are glucose monitors a good thing for 2-year-olds? Experts look at risks, benefits

## As CGMs get over-the-counter approval, some clinicians welcome new tool and others worry about disordered eating

- [Manage alerts for this article](https://www.statnews.com/my-account/edit/#emails)
- [Email this article](mailto:?subject=Experts%20weigh%20risks,%20benefits%20of%20OTC%20glucose%20monitors%20for%20children&body=Experts%20weigh%20risks,%20benefits%20of%20OTC%20glucose%20monitors%20for%20children%0A%0AFDA%20approval%20of%20Dexcom%27s%20Stelo%20over-the-counter%20CGM%20offers%20new%20approach%20to%20tackling%20childhood%20obesity,%20but%20also%20a%20risk%20of%20disordered%20eating.%0A%0Ahttps://www.statnews.com/2026/07/08/dexcom-stelo-cgm-risk-benefit-otc-glucose-monitoring-children/)

![](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/AdobeStock_2053011416-645x645.jpeg)Adobe

By [Lauren Chan](https://www.statnews.com/staff/lauren-chan/)

July 8, 2026

AAAS Mass Media Science & Engineering Fellow

[Lauren Chan](https://www.statnews.com/staff/lauren-chan/)

[lauren.chan@statnews.com](mailto:lauren.chan@statnews.com "Email")

Lauren writes about healthcare AI data interoperability, the science behind public health recommendations, and pharmaceutical advances in the rare disease space.

[Subscribe](https://www.statnews.com/stat-plus/) or [log in](https://www.statnews.com/login/) to STAT+ to unlock email alerts

You get a CGM, you get a CGM, everybody gets a CGM? For the first time in the United States, children 2 and older can also get a continuous glucose monitor without a doctor’s prescription.

Is that a good thing? The Food and Drug Administration thinks so.

Advertisement

“By providing real-time glucose data, these devices can help pediatric patients and their caregivers build greater glycemic awareness, track patterns in response to meals and exercise, and make informed adjustments to support healthier long-term outcomes and quality of life,” the [agency wrote](https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-clears-first-over-counter-continuous-glucose-monitor-children) last month when clearing Dexcom’s Stelo for marketing.

title: The AI licensure debate is missing the point of licensure
summary: The article argues that medical licensure serves a purpose far beyond passing a standardized test—it establishes individual accountability for clinical decisions. Authors Afnan R. Tariq and Ami Bhatt use a hypothetical scenario in which a cardiologist overrides an AI-recommended diagnosis to illustrate the core problem: when an AI-assisted clinical decision goes wrong, responsibility falls on the treating physician whose name appears in the chart, not on the algorithm's developer or the deploying health system. The piece contends that the growing licensure debate for medical AI is fundamentally misframed, because physician accountability exists regardless of whether the clinician personally selected or trained the underlying algorithm. This framing carries direct implications for how AI diagnostics should be regulated and who bears legal and professional liability when AI tools are deployed in clinical settings.

Opinion [First Opinion](https://www.statnews.com/category/first-opinion/)

# The AI licensure debate is missing the point of licensure

## Licensing is about much more than passing a test

- [Manage alerts for this article](https://www.statnews.com/my-account/edit/#emails)
- [Email this article](mailto:?subject=Physicians%20have%20to%20answer%20for%20medical%20AI%E2%80%99s%20decisions&body=Physicians%20have%20to%20answer%20for%20medical%20AI%E2%80%99s%20decisions%0A%0AAI%20will%20reshape%20how%20clinical%20decisions%20are%20formed.%20The%20question%20is%20who%20answers%20for%20the%20decision%20based%20on%20that%20output.%0A%0Ahttps://www.statnews.com/2026/07/08/licensing-ai-medicine-physician-responsibility/)
- [Share this article](https://www.statnews.com/2026/07/08/licensing-ai-medicine-physician-responsibility/#)

![](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/AdobeStock_830874277-645x645.jpeg)Adobe

By Afnan R. Tariqand Ami Bhatt

July 8, 2026

Tariq is co-chair of the SCAI Artificial Intelligence Task Force, and a CPT and RUC adviser. Bhatt serves as chief innovation officer of the American College of Cardiology and chair of the FDA Digital Health Advisory Committee.

A cardiologist reviews an echocardiogram flagged by an algorithm she did not choose, trained on data she has never seen, deployed by a health system that did not ask for her input. The algorithm recommends a diagnosis. She disagrees. She overrides it. The patient does well.

No one will remember this moment. But if she had acquiesced and the patient suffered harm, she would be the one in the deposition, with her license on the line. Not the engineer who built the algorithm. Not the vendor who sold it. Not the health system that deployed it.

Advertisement

Her name in the chart. Her judgment. Her license. _Her_.

title: Trump administration pursues more durable changes to science policy after setbacks in court
summary: The Trump adminstration is advancing a new Office of Management and Budget (OMB) proposal to codify permanent OMB oversight authority over federally funded research—a more legally durable approach after earlier, more overt policy actions were struck down in federal court for violating the Administrative Procedure Act. During its first year, the administration fired thousands of federal civil servants, terminated an unprecedented number of scientific projects from prior administrations, pressured universities to abandon diversity programs, and attempted a 15% cap on indirect research costs that all faced swift legal challenges. Georgetown law professor Andrew Twinamatsiko characterized last year's disruptions as "tempests we could weather," suggesting scholars expected reversals under a future administration. The OMB proposal signals an effort to entrench control over research direction through budgetary authority rather than agency-level rulemaking, which proved more legally vulnerable.
analysis_notes: Full article body is behind STAT+ paywall. Analysis relies on 2 visible intro paragraphs, headline/subheadline, author bio (Anil Oza focuses on NIH/health equity), topic tags (Donald Trump, NIH, research, STAT+), and related article links to infer the story. The piece appears to cover how the Trump admin is adapting its science policy strategy after earlier actions were blocked by courts per APA violations—shifting from dismantling agencies to centralizing OMB control over research funding.

# Trump administration pursues more durable changes to science policy after setbacks in court

## OMB proposal seeks to set in stone its control over research

* * *

By [Anil Oza](https://www.statnews.com/staff/anil-oza/)

July 8, 2026

![](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Anil-Oza-avatar-round-80x80.png)

[Anil Oza](https://www.statnews.com/staff/anil-oza/)

1. [X](https://x.com/@AnilOza16 "X")

[anil.oza@statnews.com](mailto:anil.oza@statnews.com "Email")

Anil Oza is a general assignment reporter at STAT focused on the NIH and health equity. You can reach him on Signal at aniloza.16.

[Get email alerts for Anil OzaSigned up for email alertsRemove email alerts](https://www.statnews.com/2026/07/08/trump-research-funding-scientists-see-omb-overreach-control/#)

[Subscribe](https://www.statnews.com/stat-plus/) or [log in](https://www.statnews.com/login/) to STAT+ to unlock email alerts

- [Manage alerts for this article](https://www.statnews.com/my-account/edit/#emails)
- [Email this article](mailto:?subject=Lessons%20learned:%20OMB%20changes%20tactics%20in%20control%20of%20science&body=Lessons%20learned:%20OMB%20changes%20tactics%20in%20control%20of%20science%0A%0AAfter%20legal%20challenges%20succeeded,%20Trump%20administration%20shifts%20from%20dismantling%20agencies%20to%20centralizing%20OMB%20control%20over%20research%20funding.%0A%0Ahttps://www.statnews.com/2026/07/08/trump-research-funding-scientists-see-omb-overreach-control/)
- [Share this article](https://www.statnews.com/2026/07/08/trump-research-funding-scientists-see-omb-overreach-control/#)

TThousands of federal civil servants who academic researchers see as partners in conducting their work were [fired](https://www.statnews.com/2025/04/11/hhs-layoffs-analysis-health-impact-nih-cuts-cdc-rif-fda-reduction-in-force/). An unprecedented number of scientific projects funded by previous administrations were [terminated](https://www.statnews.com/2025/12/15/nih-specifies-how-grant-reviewers-should-ensure-alignment-with-trump-priorities/). Universities were pressured to abandon diversity programs and work to curb health disparities. On a Friday evening, the government tried to push through a dramatic change to how it [reimburses universities for research overhead](https://www.statnews.com/2025/02/07/nih-slashes-indirect-costs-on-all-grants-to-15-percent-trump/).

All of these actions in the first year of the Trump administration were rapidly challenged in federal court, in many cases resulting in the administration having to walk back policies because they ran afoul of the Administrative Procedures Act, which governs how new policies and regulations are rolled out.

Advertisement

Andrew Twinamatsiko, who is director of the Center for Health Policy and the Law at Georgetown University and runs a [health care litigation tracker](https://litigationtracker.law.georgetown.edu/), describes what happened last year as “tempests that we could weather” until there’s a new administration, when “there can be ways of reverting back to the baseline that we used to have.”
